1:
The chapter opens on the boy-king Josiah, and the witnesses set his eight-year accession within the sweep of the reformation the whole chapter serves. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch stand back to survey the design: after a brief account of the length and spirit of the reign in vv.1-2, the historian gathers into one grand picture everything Josiah did for the restoration of true worship, and dates the whole reform to the eighteenth year because it was then that the book of the law was discovered, through which the reformation was carried to completion — a chronological datum he places at the head and repeats at the close. Under that one heading fall the repairing of the temple, the finding of the book, its reading to the king, and the inquiry made of the prophetess Huldah.

The reformed John Gill does the domestic arithmetic: Josiah was but eight years old, and must have been born when his father Amon was only sixteen, since Amon lived but twenty-four years. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets him at the summit of the line: succeeding Amon and reigning thirty-one years, Josiah was one of the best, if not the best, of all the Jewish kings since David, for "he began well, continued well, and ended well." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the philology and the geography: the name Josiah seems to mean "Jah healeth" (he compares Exod. 15:26 and Isa. 30:26); the queen-mother was probably paramount in the government during the first years of so young a reign; Bozkath, his mother's town, lay in the lowland of Judah (Josh. 15:39); and "thirty and one years," he notes, means that and somewhat over.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the boy's age into a marvel of grace. "Woe unto thee, O land, when thy king is a child; but happy art thou, O land, when thy king is such a child." Josiah, being young, had not yet been hardened by the example of his father and grandfather; he soon saw their errors and took warning by them, and Henry points to Ezek. 18:14, the son who sees his father's sins and does not so. Here Henry finds the sovereignty of distinguishing grace: the father passed by and left to perish, the son a chosen vessel, cut off from the wild olive and grafted into the good, as in Rom. 11:24 — "nothing is too hard for that grace to do." The witnesses do not divide; they frame the reign as the unlikely flowering of mercy in a poisoned soil.
2:
The single verse of verdict — "he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, and walked in all the way of David his father" — draws the witnesses to the wonder of a good king sprung from bad. The reformed John Gill reads the "right" first of all in the sphere of religious worship, where Josiah's rightness would most be tested, though it reached into all things besides. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fasten on the completeness of the clause: he walked in all the way of David, and "turned not aside to the right hand or to the left" — an unswerving fidelity that the whole following narrative of reform will document, and which the historian sets down as the settled character of the reign before he tells a single deed of it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the verse a text on sovereign grace. Here, he says, is the sovereignty of divine grace: the father passed by, the son made a chosen vessel; and here its triumph, for Josiah was born of a wicked father, had neither good education nor good example, was surrounded by those who would have had him tread in his father's steps, and yet the grace of God made him an eminent saint. He walked in a good way and turned not aside, for there are errors on both hands — Henry notes that God kept him from both, so that he fell neither into superstition on the one side nor into profaneness on the other. It is the same grafting into the good olive of Rom. 11:24 that Henry had celebrated over the boy's accession.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is briefest of all, simply referring the phrase "and walked" to his fuller note on the parallel in 2 Chron. 34:2, where the Chronicler gives the same testimony. The witnesses do not contend. They agree that the verse is no bare formula but the deliberate epitaph the historian writes over the reign in advance — a king measured against David and found, uniquely among the later sons of the house, to have kept the straight road without deviation. Whatever tragedy the chapter will unfold for Judah, it will not be charged to any turning-aside of Josiah; the throne, at least, was right with God, and the reform to come flows straight from a heart already set toward him.
3:
"In the eighteenth year of king Josiah" the narrative of the great reform properly begins, and the witnesses attend first to the date and then to the man sent. The reformed John Gill is careful that this is the eighteenth year not of the king's age but of his reign, and that it was by no means the first religious act of the reign — Scripture elsewhere records what Josiah did in his eighth and twelfth years, so that the sending of Shaphan marks a stage in a work long under way, not its beginning.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry accounts for the interval. Josiah had begun to seek the Lord much sooner, as 2 Chron. 34:3 shows, but it is to be feared the work of reformation went slowly on and met with much opposition, so that he could not effect all he designed until his power was thoroughly confirmed; the time lost in his minority should quicken those who have come to years to serve God with the more vigour — "having begun late, we have need to work hard." He sent Shaphan, the secretary of state, up to Hilkiah the high priest, a commission Henry compares with the parallel at 2 Chron. 34:8.

The grammatical-historical witnesses fill in the office and the difficulties. John Wesley glosses "the scribe" plainly as the secretary of state. Charles Ellicott, drawing on the fuller Chronicles account, notes that it mentions beside Shaphan both Maaseiah the governor of the city and Joah the recorder, and he defends their historicity against Thenius, who had pronounced them fictitious; only Shaphan is named here because he was the chief man in the business. Ellicott adds that the Hilkiah of this chapter (1 Chron. 6:13) is a different person from the Hilkiah who was father of Jeremiah, a priest but not high priest (Jer. 1:1) — a distinction Gill too had weighed and rejected. He observes that more than two hundred years had passed since the like repair under Joash, so the fabric might well need mending, and that Josiah, like Hezekiah before him, did not wholly succeed in rooting out the spirit of apostasy, being influenced for good by the preaching of Jeremiah and Zephaniah though reared under the opposite influence of a Manasseh and an Amon.
4:
The king's instruction is precise: "Go up to Hilkiah the high priest, that he may sum the silver which is brought into the house of the LORD, which the keepers of the door have gathered of the people." The reformed John Gill notes that Hilkiah, as high priest, had his own apartment in the temple, and returns to the question whether he is the Hilkiah who fathered Jeremiah — an identification an Arabic writer had made but which Gill judges unlikely.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reckons the years the money represents. Since Josiah had begun to seek the Lord as soon as he began to reign, the worship neglected and suppressed under his father was restored at once, and the people resumed their accustomed offerings; so that by this eighteenth year some ten years had elapsed during which the money had accumulated, no one having yet taken account of it nor applied it to the repair of the temple for which it was given. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the mandate as one long sentence running through v.8: the silver was to be paid over to the builders, and the whole period finds its apodosis only when Hilkiah, in the course of the work, announces his discovery. Charles Ellicott attends to the verb "sum," which means to make up or ascertain the amount, noting that the Septuagint read instead "seal up," a Hebrew word of which the present text might be a corruption; and he ties "the keepers of the door" back to the like arrangement under Joash at 2 Kings 12:9.

The witnesses converge on the quiet significance of the moment. The repair of the temple is the outward and unremarkable business — masons, timber, and the counting of alms — under cover of which the great thing of the chapter is about to be found. Clarke's ten silent years of accumulated offerings, Gill's high priest in his temple chamber, Ellicott's door-keepers gathering the people's gifts: all describe an ordinary work of restoration proceeding by ordinary means, the very setting in which, as the next verses tell, the long-buried book of the law will come unexpectedly to light. The historian lingers over the machinery of the collection precisely so that the discovery, when it breaks in, will fall upon the reader as it fell upon Hilkiah — in the midst of routine, unlooked-for.
5:
The mandate continues in a chain of delivery: the summed silver was to be given "into the hand of the doers of the work, that have the oversight of the house of the LORD," and by them laid out upon the actual repair. The reformed John Gill marks only the plain sense of the clause — the money passed from the high priest and the scribe to the overseers of the work, who were to disburse it. The verse is a single link in the administrative order the king had laid down, and the witnesses read it as of a piece with the trust that will be so signally praised two verses on.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the transaction in the frame of the whole repair. The money had been collected by the door-keepers a little at a time, so that the burden was insensible and, being voluntary, went uncomplained of — much the same method, he observes, that Joash had used; and now the king ordered it laid out for the repair of the temple. Henry sees in the arrangement a model of orderly zeal: the funds gathered gently from a willing people, then committed to competent hands to be spent on the sacred fabric, with the king himself taking care that the work not languish for want of means.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as part of the long royal command that runs unbroken from the sending of Shaphan to Hilkiah's announcement, its several clauses — deliver it to the workmen, let them give it to those who repair the breaches — forming one continuous instruction. The witnesses do not divide; the verse carries no controversy, only the sober record of a pious administration. Yet its very ordinariness is the point the narrative presses: while the silver moved from hand to hand and the breaches of the house were being closed, the providence of God was preparing, in that same house, the recovery of the word that would shake the king to his knees. The channel of the money is traced with care so that we may see how the treasure greater than silver lay waiting to be found along its course.
6:
The verse itemizes the trades and materials of the repair — "unto carpenters, and builders, and masons, and to buy timber and hewn stone to repair the house." The reformed John Gill distributes the labour: some were employed in mending the woodwork, others in repairing the stone walls, the whole a thorough restoration of a fabric that more than two centuries had worn, and which the heathenish princes of the intervening reigns had further defaced.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry keeps his eye on the honesty of the operation, which the next verse will commend, and reads the naming of the several workmen as testimony to the scale and seriousness of the undertaking — real craftsmen, real timber and stone, a costly and public work of piety. The temple that idolatrous kings had defaced was being made whole again, and Henry finds in the enterprise the fitting occupation of a reforming reign: those who are about the good work of repairing God's house are exactly where blessing is wont to find them, for it was in the doing of this very duty that the book of the law would be recovered.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch include the verse in the long sentence of the royal mandate, the carpenters and builders and masons being the "doers of the work" into whose hands the silver was to be delivered, and the timber and hewn stone the visible objects of the expenditure — beams to renew what had rotted, dressed stone to close the breaches in the walls. The witnesses do not divide over so plain a record. What they let the reader feel is the deliberate, unhurried ordinariness of it — the buying of beams and dressed stone, the mending of wood and wall — against which the discovery about to be announced will stand out in the sharper relief. The narrative dwells on the scaffolding of the repair, board by board, precisely because the God who orders the mending of a house is at the same moment ordering the far greater mending of a kingdom, and the book that will begin it lies, even now, somewhere within the very walls the masons are handling.
7:
"Howbeit there was no reckoning made with them of the money that was delivered into their hand, because they dealt faithfully." The witnesses gather round the honesty the verse records. The reformed John Gill explains the mechanics: no account was kept between the high priest and the king's scribe, who delivered the money, and the overseers of the workmen, who received it; the latter were called to no audit, so entire was the confidence reposed in them.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the notice as high praise of the workmen — they had gained such a reputation for honesty that no reckoning was thought needful — though he adds a shrewd qualification touching those who employed them. "A man should count money," we say, "after his own father"; it would have been no wrong to reckon with the workmen, that others also might be satisfied of their integrity. So the verse is certainly to the praise of the labourers, whether or not it is altogether to the prudence of the overseers; the honesty was real, even if the want of an audit was less than wise.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott refines the grammar. The opening "Howbeit there was" he would render "Only let there be," taking the words of v.6 and this verse to be part of the royal mandate itself rather than the historian's later comment — the king directing that the money be handed over without a strict reckoning because the men dealt faithfully. He compares the differently-turned construction of the parallel account under Joash at 2 Kings 12:14, where a like arrangement is described in another sense. The witnesses thus divide mildly over whether the clause reports a fact or prescribes a policy — Gill and Henry hearing a record of trust honoured, Ellicott hearing a term of the king's own order — but they agree on the substance: the work went forward on the strength of the workmen's proven fidelity. It is a small, bright detail in a dark history, a flash of plain integrity in the house of God just before the finding that will expose how far the nation as a whole had fallen from faithfulness.
8:
Here the chapter's turning-point arrives: "And Hilkiah the high priest said unto Shaphan the scribe, I have found the book of the law in the house of the LORD." The reformed John Gill notes that this was not Hilkiah's word at the first message but afterward, once the high priest had examined the temple and its stores to see what repairs were needed and where. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lingers over the loss and the recovery. The book had been lost or missing — carelessly mislaid and neglected, thrown by into a corner as some throw their Bibles, or maliciously concealed by the idolatrous kings, or carefully hidden by its friends lest it fall into its enemies' hands; whoever were the instruments of its preservation, we ought to acknowledge the hand of God in it. If this was the only authentic copy then in being, Henry marvels that good people did not tremble for it as Eli trembled for the ark; and he takes it, most likely, to be the very copy laid up by Moses' command in the most holy place (Deut. 31:26).

Here the witnesses raise a real question: what exactly was the book? The wesleyan-arminian voices press for the Mosaic original. Adam Clarke asks whether this was the autograph of Moses and thinks it very probable, since the parallel at 2 Chron. 34:14 calls it "the law of the LORD by Moses"; he takes it for the original of the covenant renewed in the plains of Moab and laid up beside the ark, and rejects the notion that it was the only copy in the land, citing Jehoshaphat's itinerant teaching from the book of the law in 2 Chron. 17:7. John Wesley agrees: it was that original book written by the hand of Moses, put beside the ark, hidden by some godly priest when idolatrous kings burnt the copies they could find, and now found among the rubbish.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is more cautious. The definite phrase — literally "the book of the Torah have I found" — proves that what Hilkiah found was something already known, not merely a book but the book; yet he rehearses at length how little the critics agree on its precise contents, and inclines to a collection of Mosaic statutes afterward worked up in the Pentateuch and especially in Deuteronomy, the same to which Jeremiah refers (Jer. 11:1) and which is called the Book of the Covenant at 2 Kings 23:2. So the debate stands drawn: the Mosaic autograph laid beside the ark (Clarke, Wesley, and probably Henry), or a known collection of the law, long disused and now recovered, that had been shaped into the books we possess (Ellicott). All agree on what mattered to Josiah — the book was authentic, ancient, and binding.
9:
Shaphan returns to the king with a double report, and the witnesses attend to the first half of it: "Thy servants have gathered the money that was found in the house, and have delivered it into the hand of them that do the work." The reformed John Gill notes simply that the scribe brought the king word again of how his commission had sped — the message delivered to the high priest and its execution begun.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch parse the diplomacy of the account. "To bring answer again" is to render a report on a commission received; and the verb rendered "gathered" means rather that they "poured out" the money — emptied it from the chest in which it had been collected and told it into bags — a phrase the Chronicler simplifies. They are careful, too, that the later notice "he read it to the king" does not mean the whole book was read, as a comparison with the fuller reading in the next chapter shows. Charles Ellicott confirms the point about the money: "have gathered" should be "have poured out," from the alms-chest into the bags; and "in the house" he takes in the wider sense that includes the outer court, noting that Chronicles and the versions read more fully "in the house of the LORD."

The witnesses do not divide. What they draw out is the studied ordinariness of Shaphan's errand: he comes as a faithful officer reporting the discharge of a routine duty, the counting and paying-over of the temple money, with no sense yet of the weight of what he carries. The reader, who has heard Hilkiah's announcement in the verse before, knows that the secretary's satchel holds more than an account of silver; but Shaphan's own words keep to the business of the repair. The narrative lets the great matter come almost as an afterthought to the small, so that the king first hears of accomplished bookkeeping and only then, in the verse to follow, of the book — the true treasure mentioned last, and almost in passing. So the witnesses find in the very structure of the report a lesson in providence: the errand undertaken for silver becomes the occasion of a far greater finding, and the faithful discharge of a small commission opens the door to the word that will reform a kingdom.
10:
Now the secretary comes to the greater half of his report: "Shaphan showed the king, saying, Hilkiah the priest hath delivered me a book. And Shaphan read it before the king." The reformed John Gill marks only that Shaphan "further related" what followed, moving from the account of the money to the matter of the book. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat the verse as the pivot of the whole narrative, from which the reading of the law and the inquiry of Huldah both flow; and they note that Shaphan's plain phrase, "Hilkiah hath delivered me a book," betrays how strange and unlooked-for the thing was — spoken as of a novelty, because the book had been so long forgotten.

Charles Ellicott weighs the words "read it before the king." Following Keil, he suggests that what struck Josiah so were such passages as the curses of Deuteronomy 28 and Leviticus 26; and he cautions that the phrase does not mean Shaphan read the whole volume through, for had the entire book been read we should expect the fuller expression "all the words of the book," which the next verse pointedly withholds. The reading was enough to pierce the king, but it need not have been exhaustive.

The witnesses do not contend, and together they let the scene gather its force. A king who had already spent long years in reform, purging idols and repairing the temple, is confronted for the first time — or as if for the first time — with the written law of his God, read aloud in his hearing by a court official. The plainness of the setting is itself striking: no vision, no prophet's thunder, only a scribe reading from a recovered scroll. Yet the word read is living, and the next verse will show what a single hearing of the law's threatenings can do to a tender conscience. The historian has brought his reader, step by patient step, from the counting of alms to this — the moment the word of God is set once more before the throne of Judah, and the throne trembles.
11:
"And it came to pass, when the king had heard the words of the book of the law, that he rent his clothes." The witnesses read the torn garments as the whole heart of the man laid bare. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in the gesture both shame and dread — Josiah rent his clothes as one ashamed of the sin of his people and afraid of the wrath of God; he had long thought the case of his kingdom bad, by reason of its idolatries, but never so bad as the book now showed it to be. The rending of his clothes signified the rending of his heart for the dishonour done to God and the ruin he saw coming upon his people. Now that the commandment came, sin revived and appeared exceeding sinful; in the glass of the law he saw his people's sins more numerous and more heinous than before, and inferred that great must be the wrath kindled against them.

Here the witnesses raise a genuine question: how new was this word to the king? The reformed John Gill leans hard on its novelty — Josiah, though six-and-twenty and far advanced in reform, had never written out a copy of the law as the kings were commanded, nor read the whole of it; perhaps, Gill suggests, the priests and people had till now only abstracts of the law, and the reformation thus far had consisted chiefly in abolishing idolatry rather than in restoring the whole worship, for the passover itself was ordered kept only afterward. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley demurs: it seems to him more probable that Josiah had seen and read the book before, but that the great reverence he bore the original, and its strange and seasonable finding, awakened him to a far more serious and diligent weighing of all it contained. So the two divide — was the law effectively unknown, surviving only in fragments, or known but neglected until this providential recovery quickened it?

Wesley presses the providence either way: what a mercy that the book was still preserved, and what a mercy that the whole book of God is preserved to us — "if the holy scriptures had not been of God, they had not been in being at this day; God's care of the Bible is a plain proof of his interest in it." And he notes, with Henry, the fitness of the moment: they were about a good work, repairing the temple, when the book was found, for those who do their duty according to their knowledge shall have their knowledge increased. The witnesses meet in the lesson, however they part on the history: a tender king, pierced by the bare reading of the law, becomes the instrument by which the neglected word is restored to its throne.
12:
The stricken king does not sink into despair but acts, commanding a deputation of five — "Hilkiah the priest, and Ahikam the son of Shaphan, and Achbor the son of Michaiah, and Shaphan the scribe, and Asahiah a servant of the king's." The reformed John Gill notes only that the Hilkiah so charged is the high priest already named. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify the men: Ahikam is the same who would later shield Jeremiah and whose son Gedaliah became governor after the fall of Jerusalem; the others are officers of the court and temple, sent to inquire of the Lord on the king's behalf.

Charles Ellicott enlarges the identifications from the prophetic books. He compares this embassy with the like sending to Isaiah under Hezekiah at 2 Kings 19:2; he finds Ahikam again in Jer. 26:24, where he saves the prophet from death, and Achbor in Jer. 26:22; and he takes Asahiah, "a servant of the king's," to be the aide-de-camp or "knight" who attended the king's person, an office he compares with that of the officer at 2 Kings 10:22. These are no fictitious names but men who reappear in the sober record of Judah's last decades, several of them protectors of Jeremiah.

The witnesses do not divide, and the force of the verse lies in the company the king assembles. That he should gather his highest officers — the high priest who found the book, the secretary who read it, and three trusted men of weight — rather than send a single servant, measures how deeply the reading had shaken him. He does not send to soothe himself but to know the worst, dispatching them together to seek the mind of God upon what has been heard. It is the response of a conscience that will not evade its alarm but carries it straight to the Lord. The next verse will give the king's own charge to these men, and their errand will lead to the prophetess whose word will fix the fate of Judah and the peace of her king. In the very naming of these envoys the historian quietly binds this scene to the tragedy ahead, for more than one of them will be found again standing between the prophet Jeremiah and those who sought his life.
13:
The king's charge is urgent and self-accusing: "Go ye, inquire of the LORD for me, and for the people, and for all Judah, concerning the words of this book that is found: for great is the wrath of the LORD that is kindled against us, because our fathers have not hearkened unto the words of this book." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads two questions in it — what shall we do to turn away the wrath our sins have deserved, and what may we expect and must provide for. Convictions of sin and wrath, he says, should put us upon this enquiry, and put us upon it quickly, before it be too late; magistrates especially should enquire thus for their people, and study to prevent the judgments they see hanging over them.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch infer from the very terms of the commission what had been read to the king: since he speaks of the great wrath kindled because the fathers had not obeyed "this book," it must have been the curses of the law upon its despisers — such passages as Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 — that pierced him. Charles Ellicott renders "inquire of the LORD" as "seek ye Jehovah," and finds Josiah's precise anxiety in it: he wished to know whether any hope remained for himself and his people, or whether the vengeance must fall speedily; "for all Judah" he glosses as the whole nation, whom the king identifies with their guilty fathers as one people.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley states the double question most plainly: they were to enquire what they should do to appease God's wrath, and whether the threatened curses must come without remedy, or whether there were yet hope in Israel to prevent them. The reformed John Gill adds the historical note that such inquiry would naturally be made of a prophet — of Jeremiah, say, who had already prophesied some five years, having begun in the thirteenth year of Josiah — though in the event it is to Huldah that the messengers are sent. The witnesses do not divide. They hear in the verse the cry of a genuinely awakened conscience: not the idle curiosity that would merely know the future, but the anxious faith that, confronted with the wrath revealed in God's word, asks at once whether and how that wrath may be turned aside. Josiah owns the sin as his own and his people's, and seeks not escape but mercy.
14:
The five messengers go not to a famous prophet but to a woman: "So Hilkiah the priest, and Ahikam, and Achbor, and Shaphan, and Asahiah, went unto Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum... keeper of the wardrobe; now she dwelt in Jerusalem in the college." The reformed John Gill sets her in the honourable line of Miriam and Deborah, women whom God raised up to speak his mind, and remarks that Satan later counterfeited such gifts in the heathen Sibyls, of whom the ancients told that one was a Hebrew or a Jewess. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that nothing further is known of Huldah than this passage tells; her being consulted proves that she was highly distinguished for her prophetic gift, and that no prophet of renown, such as Jeremiah or Zephaniah, was then at hand in Jerusalem. Her husband Shallum kept the wardrobe — whether the priests' vestments or the king's robes — and she lived in the Mishneh, the second quarter or lower city named also in Zeph. 1:10.

The other grammatical-historical voice, Charles Ellicott, asks the natural question — why not to Jeremiah or Zephaniah? — and answers that Huldah dwelt in Jerusalem while they did not, Anathoth in Benjamin being Jeremiah's town; her high reputation is plain, since prophets are named in the next chapter. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that the king's earnest haste sent them to the prophetess nearest at hand, Jeremiah being perhaps at Anathoth and Zephaniah perhaps not yet called; and he explains "the college" as the place where the sons of the prophets and other students of God's word used to meet and be taught.

Adam Clarke, also wesleyan-arminian, seizes the occasion for a larger reflection, and here his voice runs beyond the rest. That the high priest, the scribe, and all the king's ministers should be sent to consult an obscure woman on the meaning of the law shows, he says, that "the secret of the LORD" was not with the great officers of religion — that a pope, a bishop, or a priest may in some cases not possess the true knowledge of God, while a simple woman, having the life of God in her soul, may know his testimonies better than those whose office it is to teach them; and he cites Priestley that God distinguished several women with the spirit of prophecy to show there is no essential pre-eminence of the male sex in spiritual things. The witnesses agree on the plain reason for the choice — Huldah was in Jerusalem and of proven gift — but Clarke alone presses it into a rebuke of a self-satisfied and ignorant clergy.
15:
The prophetess answers without ceremony: "Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Tell the man that sent you to me." The reformed John Gill notes plainly that she spoke to the king's messengers, giving them the word they had come to seek. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out the shape of the whole oracle that here begins: Huldah first confirms the very fear Josiah had expressed, that the Lord's wrath was kindled against Jerusalem for its idolatry, and then divides her word in two — first, to "the man who sent you to me," the sentence of judgment upon the city and its people; and afterward, to "the king of Judah who sent to inquire," a word of personal mercy. The general phrase "the man," they observe, is chosen with intent, setting the sinner-nation's doom apart from the reprieve promised to the penitent king.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fastens on that same blunt address and draws the lesson home. She uses no compliments, he says: "Tell the man that sent you" — for even kings, though gods to us, are but men to God, and shall be so dealt with, since with him there is no respect of persons. The prophetess who might have flattered a reforming monarch instead speaks over his crown to his soul, naming him simply "the man."

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse rewards their attention precisely in its plainness. A word from the Lord is about to fall on Judah and on Josiah, and its herald is a woman in the lower city who will not soften it with courtly titles. The very abruptness is a mark of its truth: Huldah speaks as one who stands in a higher court than the king's, where rank confers no privilege and the message is not trimmed to the hearer's greatness. That she calls him "the man" is not contempt but fidelity — the same fidelity that will, in the verses following, deal faithfully both the terror and the comfort of God. It is no small thing that the LORD, having a word for the throne of Judah, should send it not by a courtier but by a prophetess who owes the king nothing and fears him not at all; the truth of the message is guaranteed by the plainness of its bearer.
16:
The sentence of judgment falls: "Thus saith the LORD, Behold, I will bring evil upon this place, and upon the inhabitants thereof, even all the words of the book which the king of Judah hath read." The reformed John Gill unfolds the twofold stroke — destruction to the place and captivity to its inhabitants — the very desolation the law had threatened now decreed. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the oracle as the promise that the Lord will bring upon Jerusalem all the punishments with which the rebellious and the idolaters are threatened in the book of the law: not a new penalty invented, but the written curses at last enforced.

Charles Ellicott attends to the wording. "I will bring evil upon" he renders more exactly "I am about to bring evil unto," and notes that instead of "unto" the Septuagint, the Vulgate, and Chronicles rightly read "upon," as the next clause requires. And he presses the phrase "which the king of Judah hath read": the book had been read to him, as the Chronicler explains, so that this free way of speaking warns us against pressing too hard the earlier words "he read it" — the king is said to read what was read in his hearing. The judgment is measured out to the very letter of the book: "even all the words" of it are to be made good.

The witnesses do not divide. What they draw from the verse is the terrible fidelity of God to his own word. The book that had lain forgotten is not a dead letter; every threat inscribed in it against a covenant-breaking people is now to be executed upon the very place where it was found. Josiah had feared as much when he rent his clothes, and Huldah confirms the fear without abatement: the evil is certain, and it is nothing other than what the book itself had spoken. The word of God, so long neglected, proves mightier than the neglect; slighted as a rule, it will yet be fulfilled as a sentence. Only after this unflinching confirmation of doom will the oracle turn, in the verses to come, to the single note of mercy reserved for the king who trembled.
17:
The ground of the wrath is named: "Because they have forsaken me, and have burned incense unto other gods, that they might provoke me to anger with all the works of their hands; therefore my wrath shall be kindled against this place, and shall not be quenched." The reformed John Gill glosses the forsaking as the abandonment of God's worship, his word and his ordinances — the Targum, he notes, reads it of forsaking "my worship." The apostasy is not mere neglect but the active transfer of devotion to other gods.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke hears in "my wrath shall be kindled" the finality of a decree gone forth: Jerusalem shall be delivered into the hands of its enemies, the people will revolt more and more, and towards them long-suffering is now useless — the wrath of God is kindled and shall not be quenched. "This was a dreadful message." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the idiom. "With all the works of their hands" means with the idols they have made, the same phrase that occurs at 1 Kings 16:7; and the unquenchable fire of God's anger he links to the like language of the prophets, comparing Jer. 4:4 and Amos 5:6.

The witnesses do not divide, and together they weigh the justice of the sentence. The evil decreed in the verse before is here shown to be no arbitrary stroke but the answer to a settled provocation: a people that has forsaken the living God and burned its incense to the works of its own hands has kindled, by its own act, the fire that will consume it. Clarke's word "dreadful" hangs over the whole. Yet even here the reformers among the witnesses note the moral order in it — the wrath is "kindled," a fire the people themselves have lit, and it "shall not be quenched" because the provocation has been so long and so obstinate. The verse is the darkest in the chapter, the full and unquenchable anger of God against idolatry; and it is precisely against this blackness that the mercy shown to one tender heart, in the verses next, will shine the brighter.
18:
The oracle turns, and with it the whole tone of Huldah's word: "But to the king of Judah, which sent you to inquire of the LORD, thus shall ye say to him." The reformed John Gill marks the shift in a phrase — this second part of the message respects the king himself, what may concern him in particular, as distinct from the general doom just pronounced upon the place and people. The word that was for "the man" now becomes a word for Josiah.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had already drawn out the deliberate structure of which this verse is the hinge. Huldah's announcement falls into two halves: the first, addressed to "the man who has sent you to me," proclaims that God will bring upon Jerusalem all the punishments threatened in the book of the law; the second, addressed here to "the king of Judah who has sent to inquire of the LORD," promises that on account of his sincere repentance and humbling of himself before God he will not live to see those predicted calamities, but be gathered to his fathers in peace. The change of address from "the man" to "the king of Judah" is thus no idle variation but the very seam between judgment and mercy.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse, brief as it is, carries a weight out of all proportion to its length. It is the moment at which the God who has just spoken unquenchable wrath against a nation stoops to speak tenderly to a single penitent within it. The same message that dooms Judah distinguishes Josiah; the same prophetess who would use no compliment to "the man" now has a gentler word for "the king." Here the chapter's deepest lesson begins to surface — that even when a people's judgment is sealed, the Lord marks and spares the individual heart that trembles at his word, and will not let the common ruin swallow the particular penitent. What that sparing will amount to, and on what ground it rests, the verses following will unfold; this verse only swings the door from terror toward grace.
19:
Now the ground of the king's reprieve is named — his tender heart: "Because thine heart was tender, and thou hast humbled thyself before the LORD... and hast rent thy clothes, and wept before me; I also have heard thee, saith the LORD." The reformed John Gill glosses the tenderness as a heart soft like wax and susceptible of impression, one that (as the Targum has it) "moved" or "trembled"; for God has respect to such as are of a contrite heart and tremble at his word. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the divine answer in the same key: because Josiah feared the Lord, trembled at his word, and wept before him, God heard him — so far, that is, that these evils should not fall upon the land in his lifetime.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the verse a study in the God who distinguishes the penitent. "God will distinguish those that distinguish themselves." The generality of the people were hardened and unhumbled, and so were the wicked kings before him, but Josiah's heart was tender: he received the impressions of God's word, trembled at it, and yielded; he grieved for the dishonour done to God by the sins of his fathers and his people, and feared the judgments he saw coming. This, says Henry, is tenderness of heart, and thus he humbled himself before the Lord — weeping, it may be, in his closet; but "he that sees in secret" says it was before him, and put every tear into his bottle. "Those that most fear God's wrath are least likely to feel it."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley sums the marks of that tenderness in a line — he trembled at God's word, was grieved for the dishonour done to God, and was afraid of the judgments coming upon Jerusalem; "this is tenderness of heart." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the philological support, tracing the word "tender" through the like usage of Deut. 20:8, and comparing Josiah's self-humbling with the behaviour of Ahab at 1 Kings 21:27; the phrase "a desolation and a curse," he notes, is of the type Jeremiah uses at Jer. 44:22. The witnesses do not divide. They gather around one truth: that the God who had just decreed unquenchable wrath upon a nation stoops to mark the trembling of a single heart, and that the tears of a penitent king, shed in secret over his people's sin, were heard in heaven and answered with mercy. It is the tenderness, not the throne, that God regards.
20:
The word of mercy reaches its height and its limit: "Behold therefore, I will gather thee unto thy fathers, and thou shalt be gathered into thy grave in peace; and thine eyes shall not see all the evil which I will bring upon this place." The reformed John Gill will not let the promise shrink to a bare natural death, for to die and be buried as his fathers did would be no peculiar favour; he takes it that Josiah was gathered to his godly ancestors, to share with them in eternal life and happiness. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads "in peace" as "in a time of public peace," since the land would be untroubled in his day; and beyond that, Josiah died in peace with God and was by death translated to everlasting peace.

But a hard question presses on the verse, and the witnesses meet it head on: how was Josiah "gathered in peace" when the next chapter tells that Pharaoh-Necho slew him at Megiddo? Adam Clarke answers at length. During Josiah's life none of the threatened calamities fell, and no enemy disturbed Judea; and though the king was mortally wounded at Megiddo — where, Clarke urges, he ought not to have meddled in the quarrel between Egypt and Assyria — he was not killed on the spot, but his servants bore him in his second chariot to Jerusalem, where he died and was buried with his fathers, as 2 Chron. 35:24 relates. "However we take the place," says Clarke, "the words of Huldah were true: he did die in peace, and was gathered to his fathers in peace."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott resolves it differently. He grants that Josiah was slain in battle, as 2 Kings 23:29 plainly says, and takes "in peace" to be expressly limited by the words that follow — "thine eyes shall not see all the evil"; the peace promised was not a quiet deathbed but exemption from the greater calamity of witnessing his people's ruin. He notes too that some manuscripts read "thy graves," pointing to the burial-place Manasseh had formed at 2 Kings 21:18, with its many chambers. So the witnesses part on the manner of the fulfilment — Clarke insisting the king literally died at peace in Jerusalem, Ellicott and Wesley conceding the violent end but reading the "peace" as the mercy of not living to see the desolation — yet they are wholly one on the substance. Josiah's penitence bought him no exemption from death in battle, but it bought him this: he was taken away from the evil to come, gathered to his fathers before the storm broke, at peace with the God whose word had broken his heart. The chapter that began with a boy on the throne ends with a promise that reaches past the grave.
